systems.name: Harmonic Formula Traversal System
systems.summary: The central system of the book. Greene organizes the fingerboard as a set of named harmonic formulas — I vi ii V, iii vi ii V, iii7 VI7 ii7 V7, the diatonic cycle of 4ths, III7 VI7 II7 V7, and their variants — each treated as a reusable compositional and improvisational template. The system's traversal logic is built on two complementary movement principles: (1) stepwise diatonic voice leading, where each voice ascends or descends to the next scale-degree of the same name, and (2) cycle-of-4ths root motion, where each root sits a fourth above the previous. String transference is the guitar-specific mechanism that extends these movements beyond the physical range of a single string group. The modification rules govern voicing density — omitting 5ths or roots is explicitly permitted — and prescribe that every formula must be explored across the full fingerboard in multiple keys. The ear is the final authority on register and transposition decisions.

members.name: I vi ii V (and iii vi ii V)
members.summary: Foundational diatonic turnaround; most common opener formula.

members.name: I iii IV V (I)
members.summary: First formula introduced; teaches extensions and the triad-symbol convention for labeling formulas.

members.name: Diatonic Chord Scale
members.summary: Ordered sequence of chords built exclusively from major-scale notes, each voice moving stepwise to the next scale degree of the same name.

members.name: Diatonic Cycle of 4ths (I IV vii° iii vi ii V I)
members.summary: Each chord root a fourth higher than the previous; reducible to two alternating descending chord scales.

members.name: iii7 VI7 ii7 V7 (and I VI7 ii7 V7)
members.summary: Introduction of chromatic dominant substitution into an otherwise diatonic formula.

members.name: iii7 vi7 II7 V7 (and I vi7 II7 V7)
members.summary: Derived from iii7 vi7 ii7 V7 by raising the 3rd of the ii7 chord by one fret.

members.name: III7 VI7 II7 V7
members.summary: Fully chromaticized formula using altered dominant 7th chords throughout; the final formula in the book.

traversal_rules.name: Stepwise Diatonic Voice Motion
traversal_rules.summary: Each voice in a chord moves up (or down) to the next note of the major scale bearing the same chord-tone name, producing the next diatonic chord in the scale.

references.topic: Building a chord scale by stepwise voice motion
references.quote_excerpt: MOVING EACH NOTE IN THE CHORD UP TO THE NEXT NOTE

traversal_rules.name: Cycle-of-4ths Root Motion
traversal_rules.summary: Each chord root is a diatonic fourth higher than the previous root; the cycle is reducible to two alternating descending chord scales.

references.quote_excerpt: each chord root is a 4th higher than the previous
two chord scales descending, while they alternate with each other

traversal_rules.name: String Transference — Moving to Higher Strings
traversal_rules.summary: Relocate a chord voicing to the next higher string group: retain shape, raise any note landing on the 2nd string by one fret, then drop the whole chord five frets.

references.topic: String transference — moving to higher strings
references.quote_excerpt: move all the notes in the chord over to the next higher group of strings

traversal_rules.name: String Transference — Moving to Lower Strings
traversal_rules.summary: Relocate a chord voicing to the next lower string group: retain shape, lower any note landing on the 3rd string by one fret, then raise the whole chord five frets.

references.topic: String transference — moving to lower strings
references.quote_excerpt: move all the notes in the chord over to the next lower group of strings

traversal_rules.name: Whole-Fingerboard Exploration via Key Transposition
traversal_rules.summary: Every harmonic formula must be explored across the entire fingerboard from multiple angles; key changes prevent monotony.

references.quote_excerpt: exploring the entire fingerboard from many different angles
changing the key can be like a breath of fresh air
practice each cycle in all octaves on each string set

traversal_rules.name: V7 to I Resolution (Standing Cadential Rule)
traversal_rules.summary: V7 chords must periodically resolve to I to maintain functional-harmonic grounding; rule recurs across chapters 7, 9, 10, 11.

references.quote_excerpt: You should always try resolving the V to I
Remember to resolve the V7's to I's occasionally
Remember to try resolving the V7's to I, occasionally
Don't forget to occasionally try resolving the V7's to I's

traversal_rules.name: Bidirectional Chord Scale Practice
traversal_rules.summary: Every chord scale must be practiced descending as well as ascending.

references.quote_excerpt: Remember to go down in all your chord scales, too

traversal_rules.name: Cycle Start-Degree Flexibility
traversal_rules.summary: Diatonic cycles and chord-scale patterns may begin on any scale degree; the interval motion defines the pattern, not the starting point.

references.quote_excerpt: common for the cycle to begin on other chords than the I
patterns using chord scales can start on any degree of a scale

modification_rules.name: Omission of 5th or Root Allowed
modification_rules.summary: The 5th or root may be omitted from any voicing; ear-appeal and smooth voice leading take priority over theoretical completeness.

references.quote_excerpt: missing tones are either the 5th or the root
many chord forms leave out 5ths or roots

modification_rules.name: Ear-Governed Register / Transposition
modification_rules.summary: Register and transposition decisions are governed by ear judgment; muddy low voicings are to be avoided.

references.quote_excerpt: let your ears be the guide in determining how low

modification_rules.name: Chord Scale Decoration (Delay / Moving Lines / Team Concept)
modification_rules.summary: Chord scales may be decorated by delaying notes, by moving lines built from diatonic scale tones, or by Van Eps's team concept of alternating two interesting intervals.

references.quote_excerpt: decorate them... such as: 1) delaying notes
finding two interesting intervals in a chord and alternating them

references.topic: Decoration — moving lines from diatonic tones
references.quote_excerpt: Using moving lines built from other diatonic scale tones

modification_rules.name: Voicing Note Omission for Physical Playability
modification_rules.summary: Notes may be omitted or changed when guitar-specific physical constraints make the full voicing impractical.

references.quote_excerpt: leave a note out of a chord due to the physical problem

modification_rules.name: Cycle Combination to Avoid Overuse
modification_rules.summary: The cycle of 4ths should be combined with other cycle patterns rather than used in isolation.

references.quote_excerpt: cycle of 4ths is a beautiful harmonic tool, but it can be monotonous if overused

systems.name: Dominant Substitution Lattice System
systems.summary: The book's secondary system governs how minor-seventh chords are replaced with dominant-seventh chords and how those dominants are then altered. The foundational rule — stated in capitals — is that any m7-type chord may be replaced with a dominant 7th according to personal taste. Greene teaches a specific derivation mechanism: raise the 3rd of a ii7 shape by one fret to produce II7; when two 3rds are present, raise the lower-pitched one, typically yielding II7#9. The same mechanism applies to converting vi7 to VI7. The system permits iii7 in place of III7 and vi7 in place of VI7 throughout the altered-dominant formula. Altered dominants — voicings incorporating #9, b9, #5, or b5 — are introduced only after diatonic preparation. A boundary condition limits the 3rd-raising mechanism: it does not apply when a iim7/11 chord has its 11th in the soprano.

members.name: Minor Seventh Type Chord (m7)
members.summary: Diatonic minor seventh chords (iii7, vi7, ii7); the source class for dominant substitution.

members.name: Dominant Seventh Type Chord — Diatonic Preparation
members.summary: Unaltered dominant seventh chords used before introducing altered dominants; labeled uppercase (II7, III7, VI7, V7).

members.name: Altered Dominant Seventh Type Chord
members.summary: Dominant seventh chords with #9, b9, #5, or b5 extensions; the chromatic end of the substitution lattice.

members.name: ii as Substitute for IV (Homonym Equivalence)
members.summary: ii can substitute for IV because they share the same tones (e.g., F#m7 = A6).

traversal_rules.name: Diatonic Preparation Before Altered Dominant Introduction
traversal_rules.summary: Altered dominant chords should be introduced only after preparing the ear with diatonic 'major scaley' sounds.

references.quote_excerpt: ear will accept altered chords more easily if prepared with sounds of a more normal, diatonic
combining the consonant and dissonant types of progressions

modification_rules.name: Dominant-for-Minor Substitution (Universal Rule)
modification_rules.summary: Any m7-type chord may be replaced with a dominant 7th-type chord according to personal taste.

references.quote_excerpt: ANY m7 TYPE CHORD MAY BE REPLACED WITH A DOMINANT 7th TYPE CHORD
E7 is replacing the normal Em7

modification_rules.name: 3rd-Raising Conversion: ii7 to II7 (and II7#9)
modification_rules.summary: Raise the 3rd of a ii7 voicing by one fret to convert it to II7; when two 3rds are present, raise the lower one, typically producing II7#9.

references.topic: iii7 vi7 II7 V7 derivation method
references.quote_excerpt: raise the 3rd (one fret higher) in the ii7 types, thereby making them II7's
raising the 3rd that is the lower in pitch... II7#9 chord

modification_rules.name: Soprano Exception: iim7/11 with 11th in Soprano
modification_rules.summary: The standard 3rd-raising conversion does not apply when a iim7/11 chord has its 11th as the highest voice.

references.quote_excerpt: iim7/11 with its 11th in the soprano then none of these ideas will work

modification_rules.name: Diatonic-for-Chromatic Substitution: iii7 for III7, vi7 for VI7
modification_rules.summary: In III7 VI7 II7 V7, iii7 may be used in place of III7 types and vi7 in place of VI7 types.

references.quote_excerpt: try using iii7 types in place of III7 types, or vi7 types in place of VI7 types

modification_rules.name: Altered Dominant Color Tone Introduction (#9, b9, #5, b5)
modification_rules.summary: Altered dominant voicings incorporate at least one of the four characteristic color tones (#9, b9, #5, b5).

references.quote_excerpt: altered dominants are those with #9's, b9's, #5's, and b5's
VI7 sounds good with any altered tone when used in smooth connection

modification_rules.name: ii Substitutes for IV (Homonym/Overtone Basis)
modification_rules.summary: ii7 may substitute for IV because they share the same tones; acoustically grounded in the overtone series.

references.quote_excerpt: ii can substitute for IV because they are virtually the same chord
based on the tones of the major chord which is part of Nature's overtone series

systems.name: Fingerboard Geography and Homonym Naming System
systems.summary: The book's organizational meta-system. Greene partitions the fingerboard into five position areas used as reference anchors for all major and minor chord forms. Within each position, chord voicings are memorized by three methods — shape-based, interval-based, and root-anchored — with experienced players combining all three eclectically. The concept of chord homonyms is central: any given physical voicing may function as multiple chords depending on harmonic context. The governing rule is that context and common sense determine naming. Mastery requires knowing all useful homonym names for every chord form. This system is the prerequisite infrastructure that enables the other two systems.

members.name: Neck Area 1
members.summary: One of the five fingerboard zones used as a positional anchor.

members.name: Neck Area 2
members.summary: Second of five fingerboard reference zones.

members.name: Neck Area 3
members.summary: Third of five fingerboard reference zones.

members.name: Neck Area 4
members.summary: Fourth of five fingerboard reference zones.

members.name: Neck Area 5
members.summary: Fifth of five fingerboard reference zones.

members.name: Homonym Chord Form
members.summary: A chord voicing whose name depends on harmonic context (e.g., Em6 / C#m7b5 / A9).

traversal_rules.name: Five-Position Area Navigation
traversal_rules.summary: The five neck areas serve as spatial reference anchors; locate any chord form by identifying which area its root falls in.

references.topic: Five neck areas for chord memorization
references.quote_excerpt: Divide the neck up into five main areas for any major or minor chord

modification_rules.name: Homonym Naming by Harmonic Context
modification_rules.summary: When a chord form can carry multiple names, harmonic context of the surrounding progression determines the correct name.

references.quote_excerpt: naming of homonyms will be determined by what is easiest or just plain common sense
working with the progression Em Am... going to be called Em6
if you are working with Em7 A7 D... it will be called A9

modification_rules.name: All Useful Homonym Names Must Be Known
modification_rules.summary: A player must learn every useful name for each chord form to avoid long-term practical difficulty.

references.quote_excerpt: know all the useful names for a chord form or you will just be making it hard

modification_rules.name: Eclectic Combination of Three Memorization Methods
modification_rules.summary: Experienced players combine shape-based, interval-based, and root-anchored memorization; no single method suffices.

references.quote_excerpt: Most players use a combination of all three methods outlined here
Many guitar players learn new chords quickly by relating them visually
